id: Van 5G tot stikstofschade tien themas van 2020__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0007
text: At the end of the nineteenth century, large cities reached the limits of their capacity. Horse-and-carriage transport led to chaos in cities such as New York and London due to extreme population growth: it caused many traffic deaths and nuisance from stench, and the required animal feed placed a heavy burden on the agricultural harvest.

metadata.parent_text: At the end of the nineteenth century, large cities reached the limits of their capacity. Horse-and-carriage transport led to chaos in cities such as New York and London due to extreme population growth: it caused many traffic deaths and nuisance from stench, and the required animal feed placed a heavy burden on the agricultural harvest. Moreover, a total disruption of liveability loomed in the long term. Within fifty years London would be buried under almost three metres of manure, a journalist at The Times predicted in 1894. This gave the global capacity problem a name: [the Great Horse Manure Crisis](https://isgeschiedenis.nl/nieuws/grote-paardenmestcrisis-van-1894).

text: "Many capacity problems are the order of the day.". The manure crisis of the past has now given way to many new capacity problems.

metadata.parent_text: "Many capacity problems are the order of the day.". The manure crisis of the past has now given way to many new capacity problems. The pace of economic growth is limited by ecological constraints and other forms of scarcity. Emitting too much nitrogen harms nature, an ageing population can make our healthcare system unsustainable, and staff shortages limit companies’ ability to invest. And pushing the clock forward by fifty years produces doomsday scenarios such as a world that is unliveable due to global warming.

In both cases, it mainly concerns entrepreneurs in construction, the building materials industry, the building trade and the transport sector. “Entrepreneurs in these sectors fear that policy on nitrogen and PFAS will cost them 8 per cent of revenue in 2020. That would amount to around 16 billion euros,” says Albert Jan Swart, sector economist Industry and Transport & Logistics. **Read more on this topic in this publication: [Billions in damage expected due to nitrogen and PFAS](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/bouw/miljardenschade-verwacht-door-stikstof-en-pfas.html)**
3.

id: Van 5G tot stikstofschade tien themas van 2020__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0047
text: New generation mobile networks: 5G. Mobile networks are considered essential, strategic infrastructure for the Netherlands.

metadata.chunk_id: Van 5G tot stikstofschade tien themas van 2020__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0047
metadata.source_slug: Van 5G tot stikstofschade tien themas van 2020
metadata.source_title: From 5G to nitrogen damage: ten themes for 2020
metadata.author: Sonny Duijn
metadata.description: Legislation, consumer behaviour, sustainability. An entrepreneur has to keep many plates spinning at once. Having a good game strategy may be more important than ever in 2020. Our sector specialists already provide insight and background on ten themes that will affect companies and entire industries next year.
metadata.parent_text:
New generation mobile networks: 5G. Mobile networks are considered essential, strategic infrastructure for the Netherlands. In 2020, frequencies will be auctioned in the Netherlands for the first time for the new generation of mobile networks, called 5G. Rolling out 5G will require a major effort. 5G needs a much denser network of masts than its predecessor 4G. The installation costs of the new 5G network will therefore be higher than for 4G at the time. When it comes to auctioning 5G frequencies, a number of Asian countries and, among others, Germany, Finland and Italy have already gone before us. Planning in the Netherlands was initially delayed because one of the required frequencies was used as a Defence listening station. When the government announced at the end of 2018 that this station would move abroad, the auction could be scheduled. The auction of the first frequencies will take place in 2020. “To maintain competitiveness despite the relatively late auction, it is important that companies can benefit fully from 5G. Thanks to much higher download speeds, the new network has far greater potential for the business market than its predecessors,” says Kasper Buiting, sector economist TMT and Business Services. He foresees that 5G can play a major role in, among other things, developing the self-driving car and deploying Virtual Reality services. “Such applications can be important for the Netherlands’ future earnings model.”. **Read more on this topic in this publication: IT must drive higher labour productivity**
4.

For example, among entrepreneurs in construction and the IT sector, the shortage of staff is experienced as a strong constraint. Companies will face this in 2020 as well. The challenge for companies is to find new ways to bind staff. This can be done by offering more flexible working hours or by giving extra pay to people who are willing to work more hours. Companies can also push harder on automation to replace labour and keep growth on track. Dutch companies appear relatively reluctant to choose this route for the time being. “The mismatches vary by region; they differ by area. For example, North Brabant has a shortage of lorry drivers for domestic transport, while in Friesland there is more job search activity in this occupation than there are vacancies,” says Sandra Philippen, Chief Economist at the Economics Department. **Read more on this topic in this publication: [The labour market is larger for men than for women](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/leisure/arbeidsmarkt-is-groter-voor-mannen-dan-voor-vrouwen.html)**

metadata.parent_text: The ‘commodity trap’. Some companies get caught in a so-called ‘commodity trap’: the services or products they offer are then barely distinguishable from those of the competition, so price is the only deciding factor. The number of companies that can afford to structurally compete on the lowest price in the long term is limited. A striking example is the challenges faced in recent years by some shop operators. They have faced strong competition from online players who sell their goods at generally lower prices, often shipping to the consumer cheaply or free of charge. Distinctiveness is important to avoid being in a constant price fight.

It turns out to be difficult for some parties to differentiate themselves. Clients play logistics service providers with a weak negotiating position off against each other to pay the lowest possible price. This can lead to a price war among those service providers. As a result, the profitability of logistics companies is under pressure, making it difficult to invest in innovative services. “Threat of the ‘commodity trap’.". “It is important for logistics companies to offer distinctive services,” says Bart Banning, sector banker Transport & Logistics. “And to be able to do that, ‘leadership’ is a more important characteristic for entrepreneurs than ever. This helps to stay out of the commodity trap and to invest in innovative logistics concepts and sustainability. The transport sector must reduce CO₂ emissions and other forms of waste.”. **Read more on this topic in this publication: [Good leadership brings you new leaders](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/logistiek/goed-leiderschap-brengt-u-nieuwe-leiders.html)**
6. The changing employee and legislation. Employees are essential to a company. In earlier publications we concluded for both the retail and the [construction](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/bouw/soft-skills-sleutel-tot-structureel-succes-in-bouwsector.html) sectors that success is derived from company culture. This of course applies to other industries as well: for example, staff is crucial for [hospitality](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/leisure/verloop-personeel-kost-horeca-jaarlijks-1-4-miljard.html). Employees’ wishes have changed. Employees nowadays look more than before for a job that fits their own values, standards and beliefs. For companies it has become more important to pursue a ‘purpose’, a higher social goal. “Companies would do well to work on ‘strategic HR policy’. This can be done, for example, through upskilling and reskilling, by applying new methods of recruitment and selection, and by adjusting employment conditions to the wishes of the future,” says Han Mesters, sector banker Business Services. Companies are also being forced to look more strategically at their staff due to new legislation being introduced from 2020. New self-employment (zzp) legislation is in the making and, as of 1 January, the [Balanced Labour Market Act](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/leisure/nieuwe-wetgeving-komt-horeca-duur-te-staan.html) takes effect. The Balanced Labour Market Act aims to make permanent contracts more attractive. Employers who offer many flexible forms of work will face much higher unemployment insurance contributions.

text: Healthcare takes place more ‘remotely’. Dutch hospital care faces a difficult task. metadata.source_slug: Van 5G tot stikstofschade tien themas van 2020
metadata.source_title: From 5G to nitrogen damage: ten themes for 2020
metadata.author: Sonny Duijn
metadata.description: Legislation, consumer behaviour, sustainability. An entrepreneur has to keep many plates spinning at once. Having a good game strategy may be more important than ever in 2020. Our sector specialists already provide insight and background on ten themes that will affect companies and entire industries next year.
metadata.parent_text:
Healthcare takes place more ‘remotely’. Dutch hospital care faces a difficult task. [Profit margins in 2020](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/headlines-insights/stijging-loonkosten-stelt-ziekenhuis-voor-problemen.html) will be wafer-thin, because the rise in wage costs is only partially reimbursed due to agreements already made with health insurers. At the same time, due to ageing and the increasing number of people with a chronic condition, it is important to make investments that make care future-proof. With the rising number of patients and an acute shortage of care providers, delivering hospital care in its current form is not possible. To prevent the quality of care from coming under pressure, care must therefore be organised differently. A logical route is for hospitals to ensure that care can be provided more ‘remotely’. This can be done by offering patients explainer videos, measuring health remotely, diagnosing remotely and deploying care robots. Digitisation of the entire care process is the key. Another part of remote care is the ‘regionalisation’ of care, where a larger share of the required patient care is taken up by GPs and home care organisations. This is being put into practice by, for example, the Isala hospital in Zwolle, which expects to deliver 10 per cent of care outside the hospital within three years thanks to cooperation with care providers in the region. “It will soon become clear that it is ultimately better for the patient if care takes place more remotely on the basis of information collected at home. It can prevent a large part of time-consuming and intensive hospital visits,” says Anja van Balen, sector banker Healthcare. “The complex circumstances in 2020 may prove to be a ‘blessing in disguise’ and the transition to remote care may take further shape.”. **Read more on this topic in this publication: [Will a patient no longer visit the doctor](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/healthcare/komt-een-patient-niet-meer-bij-de-dokter.html)**
8.

Our sector specialists already provide insight and background on ten themes that will affect companies and entire industries next year.
metadata.parent_text:
Healthcare takes place more ‘remotely’. Dutch hospital care faces a difficult task. [Profit margins in 2020](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/headlines-insights/stijging-loonkosten-stelt-ziekenhuis-voor-problemen.html) will be wafer-thin, because the rise in wage costs is only partially reimbursed due to agreements already made with health insurers. At the same time, due to ageing and the increasing number of people with a chronic condition, it is important to make investments that make care future-proof. With the rising number of patients and an acute shortage of care providers, delivering hospital care in its current form is not possible. To prevent the quality of care from coming under pressure, care must therefore be organised differently. A logical route is for hospitals to ensure that care can be provided more ‘remotely’. This can be done by offering patients explainer videos, measuring health remotely, diagnosing remotely and deploying care robots. Digitisation of the entire care process is the key. Another part of remote care is the ‘regionalisation’ of care, where a larger share of the required patient care is taken up by GPs and home care organisations. This is being put into practice by, for example, the Isala hospital in Zwolle, which expects to deliver 10 per cent of care outside the hospital within three years thanks to cooperation with care providers in the region. “It will soon become clear that it is ultimately better for the patient if care takes place more remotely on the basis of information collected at home. It can prevent a large part of time-consuming and intensive hospital visits,” says Anja van Balen, sector banker Healthcare. “The complex circumstances in 2020 may prove to be a ‘blessing in disguise’ and the transition to remote care may take further shape.”. **Read more on this topic in this publication: [Will a patient no longer visit the doctor](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/healthcare/komt-een-patient-niet-meer-bij-de-dokter.html)**
8.

The climate agreement only sketches a rough route towards the 2030 climate target. The real challenge lies in actually realising all the individual projects,” says Arnold Mulder, sector banker Energy. Part of the energy transition will be enforced by the government. From 1 July 2020, the Nearly Energy Neutral Buildings (BENG) requirements will apply to new-build, and the NTA8800 requirements to all existing buildings. Due to these new requirements, energy consumption will become the leading factor in determining the energy label, instead of the presence of sustainable applications. For offices it has long applied that from 2023 they must have at least Label C and in 2030 Label A. From 2021, standards for other non-residential buildings will also be set. Regulation provides certainty, making it more attractive for companies to invest in, for example, solar panels or in thermal energy storage. **Read more on this topic in this publication: [Christmas puzzle](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/energie/kerstpuzzel.html)**

This seems easier than it is, because for many consumers price still plays a dominant role when buying food and drink. We do want to eat more sustainably and healthily, but it must not cost too much. And it still has to taste good. And that calls for creativity, says Nadia Menkveld, sector economist Agrifood. “Food producers are innovating strongly. Products with fewer or no animal proteins or sugar are gaining market share.” New ingredients are also being experimented with, such as insects or algae. It is not only food products that are being renewed; the process is also being examined. An important part of producing more sustainably is reducing food waste. Currently, one third of all food is lost worldwide. Many countries, including the Netherlands, have set the goal of halving food waste by 2030. “Producers are adapting their processes to this, but are also developing new products from food that would otherwise be destroyed.

metadata.parent_text: Entrepreneurs also face political uncertainties. One of the most topical is Brexit. After three and a half years of wrangling, 2020 will finally be the year of Brexit. After the historic victory of the British Conservatives, the UK’s departure from the European Union (EU) seems inevitable. With their vote, the British electorate have sent a clear signal that the knot must be cut after all these years and that the deal concluded by Prime Minister Johnson with the European Union in October must be implemented. Although ‘Bremain’ is no longer an option, it is reassuring that Brexit will very likely take place in an orderly fashion. A no-deal Brexit would have cost the Dutch economy 0.75 percentage points of growth in a year. The UK will formally leave the European Union on 1 February. "Some caution around Brexit remains necessary.". Even so, some caution remains necessary.

metadata.parent_text: Entrepreneurs also face political uncertainties. One of the most topical is Brexit. After three and a half years of wrangling, 2020 will finally be the year of Brexit. After the historic victory of the British Conservatives, the UK’s departure from the European Union (EU) seems inevitable. With their vote, the British electorate have sent a clear signal that the knot must be cut after all these years and that the deal concluded by Prime Minister Johnson with the European Union in October must be implemented. Although ‘Bremain’ is no longer an option, it is reassuring that Brexit will very likely take place in an orderly fashion. A no-deal Brexit would have cost the Dutch economy 0.75 percentage points of growth in a year. The UK will formally leave the European Union on 1 February. "Some caution around Brexit remains necessary.". Even so, some caution remains necessary.
